content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901           322-6441          322-6444

August 2, 1979


Mr. Edmund Duggan
San Rafael City Attorney
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA 94902

Dear Mr. Duggan:

     This is in response to your letter of July 17, 1979, and
our previous telephone conversation requesting advice on behalf
of Planning Commissioner Cliff Lund concerning the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. This
advice is provided pursuant to Section 83114 of the Act.1/
The facts as I understand them are as follows.

     Mr. Lund is the Chairman of the Planning Commission of
the City of San Rafael. As such he presided over meetings
of the Commission to consider the master plan for a residen-
tial development project which would consist of five separate
design review units or phases. He also thereafter partici-
pated in the design review for the first of five phases.
After completing the Commission's work on the first phase
and after the phase was built, Mr. Lund bought a home in the
first phase. The remaining four phases, whose design reviews
have yet to come before the Commission, are completely separate
from the phase containing Mr. Lund's home. There are left
for the Commission's consideration no question which could
affect the value of real property in the completed phase, such
as the review of open space or other areas available for
community use. Given these facts, you have asked whether the
Political Reform Act limits the permissible extent of Mr. Lund's
participation in the review of the remaining phases.


1/ All references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Mr. Edmund Duggan
August 2, 1979                                                Page Two


     Section 87100 of the Act provides that:

          No public official at any level of state or
     local government shall make, participate in making
     or in any way attempt to use his official position
     to influence a governmental decision in which he
     knows or has reason to know he has a financial
     interest.

     Section 87103 provides that:

          An official has a financial interest within
     the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
     on the public generally, on:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

          (b) Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

          (c) Any source of income, other than loans
     by a commercial lending institution in the regular
     course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
     promised to the public official within twelve
     months prior to the time when the decision is made;
     or

          (d) Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.

                                        [Emphasis added.]

     The prohibition contained in Section 87100 is the only one
which the Act applies to the performance of official functions by
public officials.  Therefore, if the decisions he faces will have
no effect on the value of the property owned by Mr. Fund, much
less a material one (and assuming of course that they will not
affect sources of income to him or business entities in which he
has an interest), there is no necessity under the Act that he
disqualify himself from participating in the decisions.

Mr. Edmund Duggan
August 2, 1979.                                    Page Three


     If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

STC:mfa

CITY
OF
SAN RAFAEL

1400 FIFTH AVE., SAN RAFAEL, CALIF 94902/PHONE: (415)456-1112

MAYOR
LAWRENCE E MULRYAN
COUNCILMEN:
GEORGE E BREINER
FRED JENSEN
JOHN A MISKIMEN
JACK G NIXON
CITY MANAGER
WILLIAM J. BIELSEPP

July 17, 1979

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Informal Opinion - Cliff Lund

Gentlemen:

An applicant came before the San Rafael Planning Commission
to master plan an area into five separate design review units.
The master plan and the first design phase was approved by
the Planning Commission and subsequently built and sold by
the applicant.

One of the units in the first design phase was purchased
by Planning Commissioner Cliff Lund. The sale to the
Commissioner was at full market price (over $100,000), and
was financed by a bank. All units in the first design phase
have been sold.

My question is: Can this Planning Commissioner now partici-
pate in the remaining approval for design phases two, three,
four, and five? Each phase has about 50 units. The units
are complete in every respect. There is no way to alter the
value of existing units (such as Mr. Lund's) by any future
approvals required in later phases.

I would appreciate your prompt reply.

[signature]
Edmund A. Duggan
City Attorney

EAD:em

sections.question: Can this Planning Commissioner now participate in the remaining approval for design phases two, three, four, and five? Each phase has about 50 units. The units are complete in every respect. There is no way to alter the value of existing units (such as Mr. Lund's) by any future approvals required in later phases.
sections.conclusion: The prohibition contained in Section 87100 is the only one which the Act applies to the performance of official functions by public officials. Therefore, if the decisions he faces will have no effect on the value of the property owned by Mr. Lund, much less a material one (and assuming of course that they will not effect sources of income to him or business entities in which he has an interest), there is no necessity under the Act that he disqualify himself from participating in the decisions.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Planning Commissioner Cliff Lund, who purchased a home in the first phase of a five-phase residential development after the Planning Commission approved that phase, may participate in design review decisions for the remaining four phases that cannot affect the value of his property.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commissioner Lund may participate in the design review of the remaining phases because those decisions will have no material financial effect on his property. Section 87100 prohibits participation only when a decision will have a material financial effect on the official's financial interests, which is not the case here.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: FPPC response letter with incoming request letter appended. Clear question and conclusion sections identifying the legal issue and FPPC's analysis under Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Government Code.

QUESTION: Can this Planning Commissioner now participate in the remaining approval for design phases two, three, four, and five? Each phase has about 50 units. The units are complete in every respect. There is no way to alter the value of existing units (such as Mr. Lund's) by any future approvals required in later phases.

CONCLUSION: The prohibition contained in Section 87100 is the only one which the Act applies to the performance of official functions by public officials. Therefore, if the decisions he faces will have no effect on the value of the property owned by Mr. Lund, much less a material one (and assuming of course that they will not effect sources of income to him or business entities in which he has an interest), there is no necessity under the Act that he disqualify himself from participating in the decisions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Planning Commissioner Cliff Lund may participate in design review decisions for the remaining four phases of a development project because those decisions cannot materially affect the value of his home in the completed first phase.
